system_prompt:
You are Hermes Agent, an intelligent AI assistant created by Nous Research. You are helpful, knowledgeable, and direct. You assist users with a wide range of tasks including answering questions, writing and editing code, analyzing information, creative work, and executing actions via your tools. You communicate clearly, admit uncertainty when appropriate, and prioritize being genuinely useful over being verbose unless otherwise directed below. Be targeted and efficient in your exploration and investigations.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

Conversation started: Sunday, May 03, 2026 03:21 PM
Model: MiniMax M2.7 Highspeed
Provider: minimax

You are on a text messaging communication platform, Telegram. Standard markdown is automatically converted to Telegram format. Supported: **bold**, *italic*, ~~strikethrough~~, ||spoiler||, `inline code`, ```code blocks```, [links](url), and ## headers. Telegram has NO table syntax — prefer bullet lists or labeled key: value pairs over pipe tables (any tables you do emit are auto-rewritten into row-group bullets, which you can produce directly for cleaner output). You can send media files natively: to deliver a file to the user, include MEDIA:/absolute/path/to/file in your response. Images (.png, .jpg, .webp) appear as photos, audio (.ogg) sends as voice bubbles, and videos (.mp4) play inline. You can also include image URLs in markdown format ![alt](url) and they will be sent as native photos.

function.name: patch
function.description:
Targeted find-and-replace edits in files. Use this instead of sed/awk in terminal. Uses fuzzy matching (9 strategies) so minor whitespace/indentation differences won't break it. Returns a unified diff. Auto-runs syntax checks after editing.

Replace mode (default): find a unique string and replace it.
Patch mode: apply V4A multi-file patches for bulk changes.

mode.description: Edit mode: 'replace' for targeted find-and-replace, 'patch' for V4A multi-file patches

path.description: File path to edit (required for 'replace' mode)

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'replace' mode). Must be unique in the file unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'replace' mode). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: Replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false)

patch.description:
V4A format patch content (required for 'patch' mode). Format:
*** Begin Patch
*** Update File: path/to/file
@@ context hint @@
 context line
-removed line
+added line
*** End Patch

function.name: process
function.description: Manage background processes started with terminal(background=true). Actions: 'list' (show all), 'poll' (check status + new output), 'log' (full output with pagination), 'wait' (block until done or timeout), 'kill' (terminate), 'write' (send raw stdin data without newline), 'submit' (send data + Enter, for answering prompts), 'close' (close stdin/send EOF).

action.description: Action to perform on background processes

session_id.description: Process session ID (from terminal background output). Required for all actions except 'list'.

data.description: Text to send to process stdin (for 'write' and 'submit' actions)

timeout.description: Max seconds to block for 'wait' action. Returns partial output on timeout.

offset.description: Line offset for 'log' action (default: last 200 lines)

limit.description: Max lines to return for 'log' action

function.name: read_file
function.description: Read a text file with line numbers and pagination. Use this instead of cat/head/tail in terminal. Output format: 'LINE_NUM|CONTENT'. Suggests similar filenames if not found. Use offset and limit for large files. Reads exceeding ~100K characters are rejected; use offset and limit to read specific sections of large files. NOTE: Cannot read images or binary files — use vision_analyze for images.

path.description: Path to the file to read (absolute, relative, or ~/path)

offset.description: Line number to start reading from (1-indexed, default: 1)

limit.description: Maximum number of lines to read (default: 500, max: 2000)

function.name: search_files
function.description:
Search file contents or find files by name. Use this instead of grep/rg/find/ls in terminal. Ripgrep-backed, faster than shell equivalents.

Content search (target='content'): Regex search inside files. Output modes: full matches with line numbers, file paths only, or match counts.

File search (target='files'): Find files by glob pattern (e.g., '*.py', '*config*'). Also use this instead of ls — results sorted by modification time.

pattern.description: Regex pattern for content search, or glob pattern (e.g., '*.py') for file search

target.description: 'content' searches inside file contents, 'files' searches for files by name

path.description: Directory or file to search in (default: current working directory)

file_glob.description: Filter files by pattern in grep mode (e.g., '*.py' to only search Python files)

limit.description: Maximum number of results to return (default: 50)

offset.description: Skip first N results for pagination (default: 0)

output_mode.description: Output format for grep mode: 'content' shows matching lines with line numbers, 'files_only' lists file paths, 'count' shows match counts per file

context.description: Number of context lines before and after each match (grep mode only)

function.name: terminal
function.description:
Execute shell commands on a Linux environment. Filesystem usually persists between calls.

Do NOT use cat/head/tail to read files — use read_file instead.
Do NOT use grep/rg/find to search — use search_files instead.
Do NOT use ls to list directories — use search_files(target='files') instead.
Do NOT use sed/awk to edit files — use patch instead.
Do NOT use echo/cat heredoc to create files — use write_file instead.
Reserve terminal for: builds, installs, git, processes, scripts, network, package managers, and anything that needs a shell.

Foreground (default): Commands return INSTANTLY when done, even if the timeout is high. Set timeout=300 for long builds/scripts — you'll still get the result in seconds if it's fast. Prefer foreground for short commands.
Background: Set background=true to get a session_id. Two patterns:
  (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers).
  (2) Long-running tasks with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working on other things and the system auto-notifies you when the task finishes. Great for test suites, builds, deployments, or anything that takes more than a minute.
For servers/watchers, do NOT use shell-level background wrappers (nohup/disown/setsid/trailing '&') in foreground mode. Use background=true so Hermes can track lifecycle and output.
After starting a server, verify readiness with a health check or log signal, then run tests in a separate terminal() call. Avoid blind sleep loops.
Use process(action="poll") for progress checks, process(action="wait") to block until done.
Working directory: Use 'workdir' for per-command cwd.
PTY mode: Set pty=true for interactive CLI tools (Codex, Claude Code, Python REPL).

Do NOT use vim/nano/interactive tools without pty=true — they hang without a pseudo-terminal. Pipe git output to cat if it might page.

command.description: The command to execute on the VM

background.description: Run the command in the background. Two patterns: (1) Long-lived processes that never exit (servers, watchers). (2) Long-running tasks paired with notify_on_complete=true — you can keep working and get notified when the task finishes. For short commands, prefer foreground with a generous timeout instead.

timeout.description: Max seconds to wait (default: 180, foreground max: 600). Returns INSTANTLY when command finishes — set high for long tasks, you won't wait unnecessarily. Foreground timeout above 600s is rejected; use background=true for longer commands.

workdir.description: Working directory for this command (absolute path). Defaults to the session working directory.

pty.description: Run in pseudo-terminal (PTY) mode for interactive CLI tools like Codex, Claude Code, or Python REPL. Only works with local and SSH backends. Default: false.

notify_on_complete.description: When true (and background=true), you'll be automatically notified exactly once when the process finishes. **This is the right choice for almost every long-running task** — tests, builds, deployments, multi-item batch jobs, anything that takes over a minute and has a defined end. Use this and keep working on other things; the system notifies you on exit. MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE with watch_patterns — when both are set, watch_patterns is dropped.

watch_patterns.description: Strings to watch for in background process output. HARD RATE LIMIT: at most 1 notification per 15 seconds per process — matches arriving inside the cooldown are dropped. After 3 consecutive 15-second windows with dropped matches, watch_patterns is automatically disabled for that process and promoted to notify_on_complete behavior (one notification on exit, no more mid-process spam). USE ONLY for truly rare, one-shot mid-process signals on LONG-LIVED processes that will never exit on their own — e.g. ['Application startup complete'] on a server so you know when to hit its endpoint, or ['migration done'] on a daemon. DO NOT use for: (1) end-of-run markers like 'DONE'/'PASS' — use notify_on_complete instead; (2) error patterns like 'ERROR'/'Traceback' in loops or multi-item batch jobs — they fire on every iteration and you'll hit the strike limit fast; (3) anything you'd ever combine with notify_on_complete. When in doubt, choose notify_on_complete. MUTUALLY EXCLUSIVE with notify_on_complete — set one, not both.

function.name: write_file
function.description: Write content to a file, completely replacing existing content. Use this instead of echo/cat heredoc in terminal. Creates parent directories automatically. OVERWRITES the entire file — use 'patch' for targeted edits.

path.description: Path to the file to write (will be created if it doesn't exist, overwritten if it does)

content.description: Complete content to write to the file

function.name: write_file
function.arguments:
{"path": "/home/marco/.hermes/skills/trading/portfolio-heatmap.md", "content": "# Portfolio Heatmap

## Description
Visualizes portfolio allocation and performance as a heatmap using hierarchical treemaps. Supports crypto, stocks, and mixed portfolios. Integrates with CoinGecko, Yahoo Finance, and CSV imports.

## Definitions
- **heatmap**: A treemap visualization where tile size = position value and tile color = performance (green/red for gain/loss)
- **snapshots**: Stored JSON snapshots of portfolio state for historical comparison
- **rebalance alerts**: Configurable thresholds that trigger when an asset drifts beyond allocated weight

## Invocation
```
@portfolio-heatmap [command] [options]
```

## Commands

### `build <source>`
Build and display a portfolio heatmap.

| Argument | Type | Description |
|----------|------|-------------|
| `source` | string | `crypto`, `stocks`, or path to CSV/JSON file |

**Options:**
- `--output <path>` — Save heatmap as PNG/SVG to file
- `--currency <code>` — Display currency (USD, EUR, GBP, default: USD)
- `--title <text>` — Custom heatmap title
- `--period <range>` — Time period for performance colors: `1D`, `7D`, `30D`, `1Y`, `ALL` (default: 1D for crypto, 1D for stocks)

**Examples:**
```
@portfolio-heatmap build crypto --period 7D
@portfolio-heatmap build stocks --currency EUR --title "My Equity Portfolio"
@portfolio-heatmap build /data/portfolio.csv --output heatmap.png
```

### `snapshot [label]`
Save current portfolio state as a timestamped snapshot for later comparison.

| Argument | Type | Description |
|----------|------|-------------|
| `label` | string | Optional label for the snapshot |

### `compare <snapshot_a> <snapshot_b>`
Compare two portfolio snapshots side-by-side with delta annotations.

| Argument | Type | Description |
|----------|------|-------------|
| `snapshot_a` | string | Label or ID of first snapshot |
| `snapshot_b` | string | Label or ID of second snapshot |

### `alerts`
List active rebalance alerts and their current status.

### `alert add <name> <asset> <threshold>`
Create a new rebalance alert.

| Argument | Type | Description |
|----------|------|-------------|
| `name` | string | Alert name |
| `asset` | string | Asset symbol (e.g., `BTC`, `ETH`) |
| `threshold` | float | Drift percentage to trigger alert |

### `alert remove <name>`
Delete an alert by name.

## API Endpoints

### Internal Skill API
```
# Get portfolio data (used internally by heatmap builder)
skill_portfolio_heatmap_get_data(source: str, currency: str) -> dict

# Trigger alert check
skill_portfolio_heatmap_check_alerts() -> list[dict]
```

### External Data Sources
| Service | Endpoint Pattern | Auth |
|---------|------------------|------|
| CoinGecko | `https://api.coingecko.com/api/v3/` | API key (free tier: 10-50 calls/min) |
| Yahoo Finance | `https://query1.finance.yahoo.com/v8/finance/` | OAuth / cookie (unofficial) |
| Alpha Vantage | `https://www.alphavantage.co/query` | API key (free: 500/day) |

## Configuration

```json
{
  "portfolio_heatmap": {
    "default_currency": "USD",
    "default_period": "1D",
    "color_scheme": "classic",
    "snapshots_dir": "~/.hermes/skills/trading/snapshots",
    "alerts": [],
    "data_sources": {
      "crypto": {
        "provider": "coingecko",
        "api_key_env": "COINGECKO_API_KEY"
      },
      "stocks": {
        "provider": "yahoo",
        "cookie_env": "YAHOO_COOKIE"
      }
    }
  }
}
```

## Code Examples

### Python: Build a Crypto Heatmap
```python
import requests
import squarify
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.patches as mpatches
import os

COINGECKO_BASE = "https://api.coingecko.com/api/v3"
API_KEY = os.getenv("COINGECKO_API_KEY")

def get_portfolio_data(holdings: list[dict], currency: str = "usd") -> list[dict]:
    """
    Fetch current prices and calculate portfolio data.
    holdings: [{"id": "bitcoin", "symbol": "btc", "amount": 0.5}, ...]
    """
    ids = [h["id"] for h in holdings]
    params = {
        "ids": ",".join(ids),
        "vs_currencies": currency,
        "include_24hr_change": True,
    }
    headers = {"x-cg-demo-api-key": API_KEY} if API_KEY else {}
    
    resp = requests.get(
        f"{COINGECKO_BASE}/simple/price",
        params=params,
        headers=headers,
        timeout=10
    )
    resp.raise_for_status()
    prices = resp.json()
    
    portfolio = []
    total_value = 0.0
    for h in holdings:
        id_ = h["id"]
        price = prices[id_].get(currency, 0)
        change_24h = prices[id_].get(f"{currency}_24h_change", 0)
        value = h["amount"] * price
        total_value += value
        portfolio.append({
            "id": id_,
            "symbol": h["symbol"].upper(),
            "amount": h["amount"],
            "price": price,
            "value": value,
            "change_24h": change_24h,
            "weight": 0.0,  # calculated below
        })
    
    for asset in portfolio:
        asset["weight"] = (asset["value"] / total_value) * 100
    
    return portfolio

def build_heatmap(portfolio: list[dict], title: str = "Portfolio Heatmap",
                  output_path: str = None) -> str:
    """
    Render a treemap heatmap. Tile size = value, tile color = 24h change.
    """
    if not portfolio:
        return "No portfolio data to display."
    
    values = [a["value"] for a in portfolio]
    labels = [f"{a['symbol']}\n${a['value']:,.0f}\n{a['change_24h']:+.1f}%" for a in portfolio]
    
    # Color: green for positive, red for negative
    colors = []
    for a in portfolio:
        change = a["change_24h"]
        if change > 5:
            colors.append("#00c853")   # strong green
        elif change > 0:
            colors.append("#69f0ae")  # light green
        elif change > -5:
            colors.append("#ff5252")  # light red
        else:
            colors.append("#d50000")  # strong red
    
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, figsize=(16, 10))
    squarify.plot(
        sizes=values,
        label=labels,
        color=colors,
        alpha=0.85,
        text_kwargs={
            "fontsize": 10,
            "color": "white",
            "fontweight": "bold",
            "ha": "center",
            "va": "center",
        },
        ax=ax,
    )
    
    # Legend
    legend_elements = [
        mpatches.Patch(facecolor="#00c853", label=">+5%"),
        mpatches.Patch(facecolor="#69f0ae", label="0 to +5%"),
        mpatches.Patch(facecolor="#ff5252", label="-5 to 0%"),
        mpatches.Patch(facecolor="#d50000", label="<-5%"),
    ]
    ax.legend(handles=legend_elements, loc="lower right", fontsize=10)
    
    ax.set_title(title, fontsize=18, fontweight="bold", pad=20)
    ax.axis("off")
    
    if output_path:
        plt.savefig(output_path, bbox_inches="tight", dpi=150)
        plt.close()
        return f"Heatmap saved to {output_path}"
    
    plt.tight_layout()
    return "Heatmap rendered"


# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    holdings = [
        {"id": "bitcoin",     "symbol": "btc", "amount": 0.5},
        {"id": "ethereum",    "symbol": "eth", "amount": 4.0},
        {"id": "solana",      "symbol": "sol", "amount": 25.0},
        {"id": "chainlink",   "symbol": "link", "amount": 100.0},
        {"id": "polkadot",    "symbol": "dot", "amount": 50.0},
    ]
    
    portfolio = get_portfolio_data(holdings, currency="usd")
    print("Portfolio:", portfolio)
    result = build_heatmap(portfolio, title="My Crypto Portfolio")
    print(result)
```

### Python: Save & Compare Snapshots
```python
import json
import os
from datetime import datetime
from pathlib import Path

SNAPSHOTS_DIR = Path("~/.hermes/skills/trading/snapshots").expanduser()
SNAPSHOTS_DIR.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)

def save_snapshot(portfolio: list[dict], label: str = None) -> str:
    snapshot_id = datetime.utcnow().strftime("%Y%m%d_%H%M%S")
    label_slug = label.lower().replace(" ", "_") if label else ""
    filename = f"{snapshot_id}_{label_slug}.json" if label_slug else f"{snapshot_id}.json"
    path = SNAPSHOTS_DIR / filename
    
    data = {
        "id": snapshot_id,
        "label": label,
        "timestamp": datetime.utcnow().isoformat(),
        "portfolio": portfolio,
        "total_value": sum(a["value"] for a in portfolio),
    }
    
    with open(path, "w") as f:
        json.dump(data, f, indent=2)
    
    return snapshot_id

def load_snapshot(snapshot_id_or_label: str) -> dict:
    """Load by ID or partial label match."""
    files = list(SNAPSHOTS_DIR.glob("*.json"))
    # Try exact ID match first
    for f in files:
        if snapshot_id_or_label in f.stem:
            with open(f) as fp:
                return json.load(fp)
    raise FileNotFoundError(f"Snapshot not found: {snapshot_id_or_label}")

def compare_snapshots(snap_a: dict, snap_b: dict) -> dict:
    """Return delta between two snapshots."""
    assets_a = {a["id"]: a for a in snap_a["portfolio"]}
    assets_b = {a["id"]: a for a in snap_b["portfolio"]}
    
    all_ids = set(assets_a) | set(assets_b)
    deltas = []
    
    for id_ in all_ids:
        a = assets_a.get(id_, {"value": 0, "change_24h": 0})
        b = assets_b.get(id_, {"value": 0, "change_24h": 0})
        
        value_delta = b["value"] - a["value"]
        pct_delta = (b["value"] / a["value"] * 100) - 100 if a["value"] else 0
        
        deltas.append({
            "id": id_,
            "value_before": a["value"],
            "value_after": b["value"],
            "value_delta": value_delta,
            "pct_delta": pct_delta,
        })
    
    return {
        "snapshot_a": snap_a["id"],
        "snapshot_b": snap_b["id"],
        "total_value_before": snap_a["total_value"],
        "total_value_after": snap_b["total_value"],
        "total_delta": snap_b["total_value"] - snap_a["total_value"],
        "assets": deltas,
    }
```

### CSV Format for Portfolio Import
```csv
id,symbol,amount
bitcoin,btc,0.5
ethereum,eth,4.0
solana,sol,25.0
```

## Pitfalls

1. **Rate limiting (CoinGecko)**: Free API tier limits requests. Cache prices for at least 30–60 seconds between calls. Use `--cache-ttl` if supported.
2. **Incorrect asset IDs**: CoinGecko uses `id` fields (e.g., `bitcoin`, not `BTC`). Always map symbols to IDs via `/coins/list` first.
3. **NFT/illiquid assets**: Squarify fails with zero or negative values. Filter out dust amounts (< $1) before rendering.
4. **Yahoo Finance breaking changes**: Yahoo frequently changes their non-public API. If ` YAHOO_COOKIE` fails, switch to Alpha Vantage or a paid data provider.
5. **Colorblind accessibility**: Default red/green scheme may be indistinguishable. Consider a diverging palette (e.g., blue-white-red) for deuteranopia.
6. **Snapshot drift**: If you rename or move assets between snapshots, comparison will miss or mismatch them. Use a stable `id` field.
7. **Currency conversion**: `vs_currencies` must match exactly — `USD` not `Usd`, `EUR` not `Eur`.
"}

function.name: write_file
function.arguments:
{"path": "/home/marco/.hermes/skills/trading/crypto-risk-management.md", "content": "# Crypto Risk Management

## Description
Comprehensive risk management for crypto portfolios. Calculates VaR, CVaR, drawdown, position sizing, Kelly Criterion, and risk-adjusted returns. Supports Monte Carlo simulation and live position tracking.

## Definitions
- **VaR (Value at Risk)**: Maximum expected loss at a given confidence interval over a time horizon
- **CVaR (Conditional VaR)**: Expected loss beyond the VaR threshold (also called Expected Shortfall)
- **Drawdown**: Peak-to-trough decline in portfolio value
- **Kelly Criterion**: Optimal position sizing formula for long-term growth: `f* = (bp - q) / b`
- **Sharpe Ratio**: Risk-adjusted return: `(return - rf) / std_dev`
- **Maximum Drawdown (MDD)**: Largest observed drawdown from peak

## Invocation
```
@crypto-risk-management [command] [options]
```

## Commands

### `var <portfolio> [options]`
Calculate Value at Risk and Conditional VaR.

| Argument | Type | Description |
|----------|------|-------------|
| `portfolio` | string | Portfolio ID or path to JSON/CSV holding positions |

**Options:**
- `--confidence <level>` — Confidence level: `0.90`, `0.95`, `0.99` (default: 0.95)
- `--horizon <days>` — Time horizon in days: `1`, `10`, `30` (default: 1)
- `--method <name>` — Calculation method: `historical`, `parametric`, `monte-carlo` (default: historical)

### `drawdown <portfolio>`
Calculate current drawdown metrics (max drawdown, current drawdown, recovery time estimate).

### `position-size <asset> <risk_pct>`
Calculate optimal position size using Kelly Criterion or fixed fractional method.

| Argument | Type | Description |
|----------|------|-------------|
| `asset` | string | Asset symbol (e.g., `BTC`) |
| `risk_pct` | float | Percentage of portfolio to risk (e.g., `2` for 2%) |

**Options:**
- `--method <name>` — Sizing method: `kelly`, `half-kelly`, `fixed-fractional` (default: half-kelly)
- `--win-rate <float>` — Historical win rate (0.0–1.0)
- `--avg-win <float>` — Average gain on winning trades
- `--avg-loss <float>` — Average loss on losing trades

### `risk-metrics <portfolio>`
Return a full risk dashboard: Sharpe, Sortino, Calmar ratio, VaR, CVaR, and drawdown.

### `simulate <portfolio> [options]`
Run Monte Carlo simulation to forecast portfolio outcomes.

**Options:**
- `--trials <n>` — Number of simulation trials (default: 10000)
- `--horizon <days>` — Forecast horizon in days (default: 30)
- `--confidence <level>` — Confidence interval (default: 0.95)
- `--output <path>` — Save histogram plot to file

### `correlation <portfolio>`
Compute and display asset return correlations. Useful for diversification analysis.

## API Endpoints

### Internal Skill API
```
# Calculate VaR
skill_crypto_risk_var(portfolio: list[dict], confidence: float, horizon: int, method: str) -> dict

# Run Monte Carlo
skill_crypto_risk_simulate(portfolio: list[dict], trials: int, horizon: int) -> dict

# Position sizing
skill_crypto_risk_position_size(risk_pct: float, method: str, win_rate: float, avg_win: float, avg_loss: float) -> dict
```

### External Data Sources
| Service | Purpose | Rate Limit |
|---------|---------|------------|
| CoinGecko | Historical price data for VaR/monte Carlo | 10–50 req/min (free) |
| CryptoCompare | Alternative price source, social data | 10 req/sec |
| Glassnode | On-chain risk metrics (advanced) | Requires paid plan |

## Configuration

```json
{
  "crypto_risk_management": {
    "default_confidence": 0.95,
    "default_horizon": 1,
    "default_method": "historical",
    "risk_free_rate": 0.04,
    "kelly_multiplier": 0.5,
    "data_source": "coingecko",
    "cache_ttl_seconds": 60,
    "alerts": {
      "var_threshold_pct": 5.0,
      "drawdown_threshold_pct": 20.0
    }
  }
}
```

## Code Examples

### Python: Calculate VaR (Historical, Parametric, Monte Carlo)
```python
import requests
import numpy as np
import os
from datetime import datetime, timedelta
from scipy import stats

COINGECKO_BASE = "https://api.coingecko.com/api/v3"
API_KEY = os.getenv("COINGECKO_API_KEY")

def fetch_historical_prices(asset_ids: list[str], days: int = 365,
                            currency: str = "usd") -> dict[str, list[float]]:
    """Fetch daily closing prices for each asset."""
    prices = {}
    headers = {"x-cg-demo-api-key": API_KEY} if API_KEY else {}
    
    for id_ in asset_ids:
        url = f"{COINGECKO_BASE}/coins/{id_}/market_chart"
        params = {"vs_currency": currency, "days": days, "interval": "daily"}
        
        resp = requests.get(url, params=params, headers=headers, timeout=15)
        resp.raise_for_status()
        data = resp.json()
        
        prices[id_] = [p[1] for p in data["prices"]]
    
    return prices

def compute_log_returns(prices: list[float]) -> np.ndarray:
    """Convert prices to log returns."""
    return np.diff(np.log(prices))

def historical_var(returns: np.ndarray, confidence: float = 0.95) -> float:
    """Historical VaR: percentile of returns."""
    return np.percentile(returns, (1 - confidence) * 100)

def parametric_var(returns: np.ndarray, confidence: float = 0.95) -> float:
    """Parametric VaR: assume normal distribution."""
    mu, sigma = returns.mean(), returns.std()
    z = stats.norm.ppf(1 - confidence)
    return mu + z * sigma

def cvar(returns: np.ndarray, confidence: float = 0.95) -> float:
    """CVaR / Expected Shortfall: mean of returns below VaR."""
    var = historical_var(returns, confidence)
    return returns[returns <= var].mean()

def monte_carlo_var(returns: np.ndarray, confidence: float = 0.95,
                    trials: int = 10000, horizon: int = 1) -> float:
    """Monte Carlo VaR: simulate future returns from historical distribution."""
    simulated_returns = np.random.choice(returns, size=(trials, horizon)).mean(axis=1)
    return np.percentile(simulated_returns, (1 - confidence) * 100)

def full_var_analysis(portfolio: list[dict], confidence: float = 0.95,
                      horizon: int = 1) -> dict:
    """
    Run all three VaR methods on a portfolio.
    portfolio: [{"id": "bitcoin", "value": 10000}, ...]
    """
    asset_ids = [p["id"] for p in portfolio]
    weights = np.array([p["value"] for p in portfolio])
    weights = weights / weights.sum()  # normalize
    
    prices = fetch_historical_prices(asset_ids, days=365)
    
    # Build combined portfolio returns
    min_len = min(len(prices[id_]) for id_ in asset_ids)
    aligned = np.array([prices[id_][-min_len:] for id_ in asset_ids])
    log_returns = np.diff(np.log(aligned), axis=1)
    
    # Weighted portfolio returns
    port_returns = (log_returns.T @ weights).flatten()
    
    results = {
        "var_historical": historical_var(port_returns, confidence),
        "var_parametric": parametric_var(port_returns, confidence),
        "var_monte_carlo": monte_carlo_var(port_returns, confidence, horizon=horizon),
        "cvar": cvar(port_returns, confidence),
        "confidence": confidence,
        "horizon_days": horizon,
        "portfolio_value": sum(p["value"] for p in portfolio),
        "max_loss_dollar": abs(historical_var(port_returns, confidence))
                           * sum(p["value"] for p in portfolio),
    }
    return results


# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    portfolio = [
        {"id": "bitcoin",  "value": 5000},
        {"id": "ethereum", "value": 3000},
        {"id": "solana",   "value": 2000},
    ]
    
    result = full_var_analysis(portfolio, confidence=0.95, horizon=1)
    print("=== VaR Analysis ===")
    for k, v in result.items():
        print(f"  {k}: {v}")
```

### Python: Position Sizing (Kelly + Half-Kelly)
```python
def kelly_criterion(win_rate: float, avg_win: float, avg_loss: float) -> float:
    """
    Kelly Criterion: f* = (bp - q) / b
    b = net odds received on the winning trade (avg_win / avg_loss)
    p = win probability (win_rate)
    q = 1 - p (loss probability)
    Returns the optimal fraction of bankroll to risk.
    """
    if avg_loss == 0:
        raise ValueError("avg_loss cannot be zero")
    
    b = avg_win / avg_loss   # net odds received
    p = win_rate
    q = 1 - p
    
    f_star = (b * p - q) / b
    
    if f_star <= 0:
        return 0.0  # No positive edge
    return min(f_star, 1.0)  # Cap at 100%

def half_kelly(position_value: float, kelly_fraction: float = 0.5) -> dict:
    """Apply half-Kelly (or custom multiplier) for more conservative sizing."""
    return {
        "full_kelly_pct": kelly_fraction * 100,
        "rationale": "Half-Kelly reduces volatility by 50% while retaining 75% of the geometric growth edge.",
        "risk_note": "Full Kelly has high variance — most practitioners use 0.25–0.50 fraction.",
    }

def fixed_fractional(bankroll: float, risk_pct: float, entry_price: float,
                     stop_loss_pct: float) -> dict:
    """
    Fixed fractional position sizing.
    risk_pct: fraction of bankroll to risk (e.g., 0.02 for 2%)
    stop_loss_pct: percentage below entry to set stop (e.g., 0.05 for 5%)
    """
    risk_amount = bankroll * risk_pct
    max_position = risk_amount / stop_loss_pct
    
    shares = max_position / entry_price  # or contracts for derivatives
    
    return {
        "bankroll": bankroll,
        "risk_amount_dollar": risk_amount,
        "max_position_dollar": max_position,
        "shares_or_contracts": shares,
        "entry_price": entry_price,
        "stop_loss_price": entry_price * (1 - stop_loss_pct),
        "actual_risk_pct": (shares * entry_price * stop_loss_pct) / bankroll,
    }


if __name__ == "__main__":
    # Example: BTC trade with 60% win rate, avg win 10%, avg loss 5%
    kelly = kelly_criterion(win_rate=0.60, avg_win=0.10, avg_loss=0.05)
    print(f"Kelly fraction: {kelly:.4f} ({kelly*100:.2f}%)")
    # Kelly recommends betting 70% of bankroll — extremely aggressive!
    # Half-Kelly: 35%
    
    # Fixed fractional example
    result = fixed_fractional(
        bankroll=10_000,
        risk_pct=0.02,      # Risk 2% of $10,000 = $200
        entry_price=50_000,
        stop_loss_pct=0.05  # 5% stop = $2,500 loss if hit
    )
    print(result)
```

### Python: Monte Carlo Portfolio Simulation
```python
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

def monte_carlo_portfolio(prices: dict[str, list[float]],
                          weights: np.ndarray,
                          initial_value: float = 10_000,
                          trials: int = 10_000,
                          horizon_days: int = 30) -> dict:
    """
    Simulate future portfolio values using Geometric Brownian Motion
    fitted to historical log returns.
    """
    asset_ids = list(prices.keys())
    min_len = min(len(prices[id_]) for id_ in asset_ids)
    aligned = np.array([prices[id_][-min_len:] for id_ in asset_ids])
    
    # Daily log returns
    log_returns = np.diff(np.log(aligned), axis=1)
    
    # Portfolio log returns
    port_log_returns = (log_returns.T @ weights).flatten()
    
    # Fit normal distribution to portfolio log returns
    mu = port_log_returns.mean()
    sigma = port_log_returns.std()
    
    # Simulate
    dt = 1 / 252  # daily step
    drift = (mu - 0.5 * sigma**2) * dt
    diffusion = sigma * np.sqrt(dt)
    
    paths = np.zeros((trials, horizon_days))
    values = np.full(trials, initial_value)
    
    for t in range(horizon_days):
        daily_returns = np.exp(drift + diffusion * np.random.standard_normal(trials))
        values = values * daily_returns
        paths[:, t] = values
    
    final_values = paths[:, -1]
    
    return {
        "mean": final_values.mean(),
        "median": np.median(final_values),
        "std": final_values.std(),
        "var_95": np.percentile(final_values, 5),
        "var_99": np.percentile(final_values, 1),
        "cvar_95": final_values[final_values <= np.percentile(final_values, 5)].mean(),
        "prob_loss": (final_values < initial_value).mean(),
        "paths": paths,
        "horizon_days": horizon_days,
        "trials": trials,
    }

def plot_simulation(paths: np.ndarray, initial_value: float, horizon_days: int,
                    output_path: str = None):
    """Plot Monte Carlo simulation fan chart."""
    percentiles = [5, 25, 50, 75, 95]
    pct_values = {p: np.percentile(paths, p, axis=0) for p in percentiles}
    
    x = np.arange(horizon_days)
    fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(14, 8))
    
    # Fan between 5th and 95th percentile
    ax.fill_between(x, pct_values[5], pct_values[95], alpha=0.2, color="blue", label="5th–95th pct")
    ax.fill_between(x, pct_values[25], pct_values[75], alpha=0.3, color="blue", label="25th–75th pct")
    ax.plot(x, pct_values[50], color="blue", linewidth=2, label="Median")
    ax.axhline(initial_value, color="red", linestyle="--", linewidth=1.5, label="Initial")
    
    ax.set_title("Monte Carlo Portfolio Simulation", fontsize=16)
    ax.set_xlabel("Days")
    ax.set_ylabel("Portfolio Value ($)")
    ax.legend()
    ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
    
    if output_path:
        plt.savefig(output_path, bbox_inches="tight", dpi=150)
        plt.close()
    else:
        plt.show()
    
    return "Plot rendered"
```

### Python: Drawdown Analysis
```python
import numpy as np

def compute_drawdowns(prices: list[float]) -> dict:
    """
    Compute drawdown series and statistics from a price series.
    """
    prices = np.array(prices)
    cumulative_max = np.maximum.accumulate(prices)
    drawdowns = (prices - cumulative_max) / cumulative_max  # as negative fractions
    
    max_dd = drawdowns.min()  # most negative = worst drawdown
    
    # Find peak and trough of max drawdown
    trough_idx = np.argmin(drawdowns)
    peak_idx = np.argmax(prices[:trough_idx + 1])
    
    return {
        "max_drawdown_pct": max_dd * 100,
        "max_drawdown_duration_days": trough_idx - peak_idx,
        "current_drawdown_pct": drawdowns[-1] * 100,
        "drawdown_series": drawdowns.tolist(),
        "peak_price": prices[peak_idx],
        "trough_price": prices[trough_idx],
        "peak_date_idx": int(peak_idx),
        "trough_date_idx": int(trough_idx),
    }

def annualized_return(prices: list[float], days: int = 365) -> float:
    """Simple annualized return from price series."""
    if len(prices) < 2:
        return 0.0
    total_return = (prices[-1] / prices[0]) - 1
    n_years = len(prices) / 365.0
    return (1 + total_return) ** (1 / n_years) - 1

def sharpe_ratio(prices: list[float], risk_free_rate: float = 0.04) -> float:
    """Annualized Sharpe Ratio."""
    log_returns = np.diff(np.log(prices))
    excess = log_returns.mean() * 365 - risk_free_rate
    return excess / (log_returns.std() * np.sqrt(365))

def sortino_ratio(prices: list[float], risk_free_rate: float = 0.04) -> float:
    """Annualized Sortino Ratio (downside deviation only)."""
    log_returns = np.diff(np.log(prices))
    downside_returns = log_returns[log_returns < 0]
    downside_std = downside_returns.std() * np.sqrt(365)
    if downside_std == 0:
        return 0.0
    return (log_returns.mean() * 365 - risk_free_rate) / downside_std
```

## Pitfalls

1. **Historical data gaps**: CoinGecko daily data may have missing days (weekends for some assets). Pad with last known value or use `/coins/{id}/market_chart` with `interval=daily` and verify data completeness.
2. **VaR assumes normal distribution**: Crypto returns are well-documented to have fat tails. Parametric VaR will **underestimate** tail risk. Always cross-check with historical VaR and Monte Carlo.
3. **Monte Carlo with GBM**: Geometric Brownian Motion assumes continuous prices and no jumps. Crypto has flash crashes and sudden pumps — consider adding jump-diffusion or regime-switching models for production use.
4. **Kelly over-leveraging**: Full Kelly (`f*`) is mathematically optimal for infinite iterations but creates wild bankroll swings. Always apply a multiplier (0.25–0.50) unless you have deep pockets and high risk tolerance.
5. **Look-ahead bias**: When backtesting, ensure you only use data available at each point in time. Future peeking inflates Sharpe ratios dramatically.
6. **Correlation instability**: Crypto correlations spike during market stress (crashes). A "diversified" crypto portfolio may not diversify when you need it most.
7. **Stop-loss execution**: `fixed_fractional` stop-loss prices are ideal — actual fills suffer slippage, especially in illiquid alts during volatility spikes. Add 1–3% buffer for realistic risk.
8. **Short data history**: Some alts have < 6 months of history. VaR on 30 days of data is statistically unreliable. Require at least 180 days for 95% confidence VaR.
"}

### `crypto-risk-management.md`
**Purpose**: Comprehensive risk analytics for crypto portfolios

**Features**:
- Commands: `var`, `drawdown`, `position-size`, `risk-metrics`, `simulate`, `correlation`
- VaR via 3 methods: historical, parametric (normal), Monte Carlo
